data.title: I Was Wrong: Bitcoin Didn't Become A Currency Of Exchange
data.description:
The first time I bought Bitcoin (BTC-USD) was in 2018, and almost eight years later, is an investment I already hold, although now it represents a very small portion of my portfolio. In any case, it has become a position I want to sell because of its high price volatility. I think it has turned into an asset that is extremely sensitive to several factors that I don't fully understand, and the recent decline led me to conclude that a new crypto winter is approaching. However, the most important reason I want to sell my Bitcoin position is that it has not met my expectations of becoming a medium of exchange or a credible alternative to the dollar.

The only legal case we know of is El Salvador, which I will discuss later. Several governments are exploring the use of Bitcoin, and this could still happen in the future. However, I was expecting a deeper and more massive use. I mean, I was expecting a more spontaneous adoption that simply didn't happen.

A 2022 report by Deloitte found that nearly 75% of retail merchants expected to accept cryptocurrency payments within two years. At the same time, the report warned about regulatory and security risks, as well as concerns over price volatility. I believe this point is crucial, as merchants are particularly vulnerable to volatility. If you had been reading that report in 2022, you probably would have imagined a much brighter future.

But today, almost four years later, a report by PayPal shows that only 39% of merchants accept cryptocurrency payments, not 75%. Moreover, the report indicates that the respondents expect full adoption to take longer, postponing that goal to 2031. But at the end, however, I believe the most important form of adoption is not by merchants but by individuals. And in this context, the picture is even more concerning: in my view, it is a very negative sign that only 1% of the volume of stablecoin shipments corresponds to real payments.

Most transactions are financial transfers, particularly movements between exchanges. What I'm trying to say is this: most Bitcoin users don't use it to simply buy a coffee, but rather as a speculative investment. Stablecoins, which I mentioned earlier, represent a meaningful attempt to reduce volatility, but I think that takes away from the essence of Bitcoin's original idea of ​​revolutionizing the traditional financial system.

The reasons behind the low usage of Bitcoin

I believe there are several key factors that blocked the use of Bitcoin. One of them is volatility: the asset's price fluctuates minute to minute, and that affects accounting for both merchants and users, especially during periods of sharp price drops.

Closely connected to that, we must mention the problem of scalability because the Bitcoin network itself has a very limited capacity to process transactions simultaneously. Because of its block size and design, the network can handle seven transactions per second. So, as more users attempt to conduct retail transactions, the slower the network could become, further complicating its efficient use.

In that case, there is a double disadvantage because in periods of high volume and rising prices, the network can become congested due to the high number of transactions in the system, making it slower and more expensive to use. At the same time, in periods of falling prices, even if transaction volume declines, using Bitcoin for trading transactions would be less advantageous: with the same fraction of Bitcoin, I'll be buying fewer products.

Other factors that affect the use of Bitcoin include the lack of clear regulation, which is essential to protect users, and the lack of security against password loss or cyberattacks. When I talk about a clear regulation, I mean a legal framework that goes beyond simply collecting taxes, as I believe is the case with the 1099-DA scheduled for implementation by the end of this fiscal year. And when I speak about regulation to protect users, I mean there isn't a well-defined compensation system similar to the protections available in the banking sector.

What I'm trying to say is this: consider losing a password or having your credit card stolen. You can request a new password from the issuing bank, as well as dispute charges after reporting theft. There is a clear process and an established protection framework. Now think about Bitcoin. It hasn't achieved massive adoption as a currency, nor has it developed a strong private protection framework, and therefore there's no way to make quick and secure claims. As I said before, stablecoins rely on the traditional banking system to prevent those issues. But the model remains similar: heavy regulation and high transaction costs. You can hold a stablecoin, but that won't prevent the loss of purchasing power of the dollar. In the following image, you can see the historical loss of value of the dollar based on CPI.

So, these problems remain key barriers to the use of Bitcoin as a medium of exchange. Since I've mentioned four determining factors, I think it's worth mentioning them: volatility, scalability issues, lack of clear regulation, and security risks. These are also the main four concerns cited by chief financial officers, according to a Deloitte survey.

The case of El Salvador

Nayib Bukele was the first president to adopt bitcoin as a legal tender, with congressional approval in 2021, although it kept the dollar as the accounting reference currency. However, at the beginning of 2025, the government reversed the mandatory nature in order to reach an agreement with the IMF for a $1.4 billion loan, which ultimately removed Bitcoin as a mandatory currency of exchange. In other words, it remains legal tender, but it isn't mandatory.

This shift revealed a much more complicated reality: Bitcoin was never used in the country; only 2 out of every 10 inhabitants used Bitcoin, according to the latest report from 2024. Bukele had envisioned Bitcoin as a tool to promote financial inclusion and strengthen remittances as a driver of private investment. But none of that happened. Only about 1% of remittances were conducted using cryptocurrencies.

That doesn't change the fact that Bukele's government continues to promote its use and manage its reserves through Bitcoin. However, recent developments are not encouraging at all. Heavy selling also affected El Salvador's reserves. Once again, its extreme volatility can lead to very negative consequences. Over the past few months, the government lost 22% of the value of its reserves. According to the data from February 16, the government held 7,564 bitcoins valued at $513 million after adding 46 new coins. However, due to falling prices, the reserves declined from $658 million. Beyond price fluctuations, my main point is that Bitcoin didn't achieve broad adoption among the population. That, in my view, is where the Salvadoran experiment ultimately fell short.

Current Factors 2026: a new crypto winter?

In addition to the obstacles Bitcoin faced in its implementation as a medium of exchange, a strong wave of selling has reinforced my concerns since the end of 2025. Bitcoin-focused ETFs have seen $7.5 billion in outflows since October. Net monthly flows have been negative for the past four months. The largest outflow occurred in October, similar to the one recorded in January of last year. You can see this trend in the CoinMarketCap chart.

This confirms a reduction in institutional positions through ETFs, which also doesn't appear to be recovering in February, with an approximate negative balance of $1.5 billion.

There are several situational factors that, I believe, are influencing the recent sell-off, and although they may be circumstantial, I see them as indirect drivers of a more structural issue: Bitcoin is not functioning as a medium of exchange, at least for now. These circumstantial factors give rise to periods known as crypto winters. From 2021 to 2024, for example, there was a crypto winter, and I think another one may be beginning.

In my view, risk aversion is one of the factors strongly affecting Bitcoin. Risk aversion has been rising due to the increased likelihood of armed conflicts and geopolitical tensions. 2026 started with the operation to remove Venezuela's Maduro from power, but now the most important is Iran. And we should not forget about the Russia-Ukraine war. That is on the geopolitical front. On the trade front (which is also tied to geopolitics), the Supreme Court ruling against the reciprocal tariffs imposed by the Trump administration via IEEPA added tension to an already adverse investment climate. In fact, Trump's rejection of that ruling and his insistence on an additional 15% tariff are what the market liked least.

Geopolitical risks are fueling other domestic tensions and threatening the Achilles' heel of Bitcoin and digital assets: liquidity constraints. The Fed's monetary policy is attempting to become more flexible, softening QT with the bond repurchase program initiated last December. I believe that decision may have come a bit late, because we must also consider the prospect that the interest rate cut cycle will be delayed longer than expected. This is where inflation comes into play, which closed at 2.9% in 2025, far from Jerome Powell's plan to approach 2%. If oil keeps rising (another geopolitical repercussion), I find it hard to imagine inflation declining, which reduces the likelihood of accelerating the rate-cutting process.

Finally, but no less important, we must take a look at the designation of Kevin Warsh as the new Chair of the Fed starting in June. This increases uncertainty because, despite Trump promoting him as aligned with his rate-cutting policy, the market is interpreting him as more hawkish. In this sense, the problem isn't so much whether Warsh is hawkish or dovish, but rather the conditions under which he will assume his position: inflation closer to 3% than 2%, elevated geopolitical risks, and a rather divided FOMC, according to the minutes released from the January meeting.

Why does all this affect Bitcoin? Because when risk aversion increases, the most volatile assets suffer the most, especially when there are no underlying industrial fundamentals, as can happen with metals. Bitcoin can function as an alternative asset in certain contexts, but I believe that today it operates more as a speculative investment than as a medium of exchange or a stable store of value.

New investment perspectives

Based on what I've said so far, my conclusion is that I'll sell my Bitcoin position. What am I waiting for? The right moment. I feel I should have sold around $120,000, and I didn't. But I'll probably sell closer to that amount if the opportunity arises. Otherwise, I may have to sell for much less or wait a little longer.

I want to make it clear that my position is not that Bitcoin can't return to all-time highs. A future scenario of greater liquidity, with much lower interest rates than today and less risk aversion, will surely help a price rebound. But I believe a completely different environment is emerging. That's why I decided to shift my investment focus toward the energy infrastructure behind Bitcoin mining and crypto mining in general.

The energy infrastructure is extremely valuable and forms the foundation of crypto networks. Bitcoin challenges in the long term were seen by crypto miners, and that's why they are trying to adapt their business to a more tangible and practical reality: AI development and HPC. Although technology companies occasionally experience price fluctuations, as seems to be happening in 2026, the reality is that AI is expanding rapidly at the business level, and demand is growing at a very fast pace.

That's why I set my eyes on crypto miners that are repositioning themselves as AI hosting providers, supplying infrastructure for the data center boom. Some of the companies I like the most are IREN Limited (IREN), Riot Platforms (RIOT), and Bitfarms (BITF). I have recently written articles on Seeking Alpha about all three companies.

From Bitcoin To AI: IREN's GW-Scale Platform Is Built For Hyperscalers

Riot Platforms: AMD Today, Many AI Players Tomorrow

Bitfarms: Capitalizing On The Next Generation Of AI Data Centers

In the more original energy sector, that is, in the companies that operate along the electricity value chain to provide electricity, I believe there are also opportunities. Many of the major utilities have contracts with hyperscalers to supply future AI data centers. Companies such as NextEra Energy (NEE), Constellation Energy (CEG) or Duke Energy (DUK) are the ones I usually follow closely. They may not look particularly cheap at the moment, but I think they are worth keeping on your radar.

Conclusions

My investment experience with Bitcoin was thrilling, but it didn't meet my expectations regarding its adoption as a medium of exchange. After carefully analyzing the asset, I found that several factors hindered this objective, including extreme volatility and technical issues that continue to discourage the general public from using it. The experiment in El Salvador, with its mandatory legal tender status, also failed to deliver meaningful results or generate broad appeal among its population.

My decision to sell comes on the cusp of another crypto winter, with the price continuing to fall from its highs of over $120,000 last October. Risk aversion, liquidity restrictions, and rising geopolitical tensions are the factors that I believe will continue to put downward pressure on the price. I'm not setting target prices because I wouldn't dare do so with a digital asset, especially one as volatile as Bitcoin. I'll simply wait for the right moment to sell my position. However, I believe that within the broader Bitcoin industry, there are attractive investment opportunities that are more stable and better suited for the long term. I'm talking about the contracts that crypto miners are securing with large technology companies as they reposition themselves to become AI data center hosts.

data.title: PUMA: Eyes On 4Q Beat And New Shareholder
data.description:
I'm still a bull on PUMA SE (PMMAF) (PUMSY) (PUMG.DE).

PMMAF's FY26 results should get better, considering the read-across from the above-consensus fourth quarter showing. The new investor ANTA (ANPDY) could have a favorable impact on its geographical expansion and product sourcing.

Q4 Numbers Weren't As Bad As Feared

The group published a set of slides revealing its recent financials on Thursday, Feb 26.

I was overly confident about PMMAF's ability to register "faster growth and margin expansion" in my prior January 10, 2025, article.

Its topline went down 27% year-on-year to €1.57 billion in Oct-Dec '25. During the same timeframe, the firm also suffered from a normalized EBIT loss of €0.23 billion.

PMMAF referred to '25 as a "year of reset" in the investor presentation. It also drew attention to various "right-sizing measures" carried out during 2H2025.

Restructuring Exercise

As per the chart presented above, it has been de-emphasizing third-party distribution and prioritizing full-price digital transactions. I can understand why B2B and online revenues dropped 28% and 20%, respectively, in 4Q on constant currency terms.

"Gross Profit Margin/GPM" deteriorated by 750bps YoY to 40.2% in the recent three-month period. Its analyst call commentary credited the lower GPM to "increased promotions in the wholesale channel and inventory reserves resulting from the distribution cleanup."

There were also unfavorable fixed-cost leverage effects. The company's "Operating Expenses" or "OpEx" was down 8% for the final quarter of last year. That's modest compared to the high-20s percent fall in turnover.

But PMMAF's quarterly turnover represented a 6% beat versus consensus. The sell-side's "Operating Profit/OP" projection was an inferior -€255 million. These figures were sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

In my opinion, certain product and geographical segments delivered positive surprises.

PMMAF's "Asia-Pacific/APAC" sales adjusted for foreign exchange effects decreased 13% YoY in 4Q25. This is better than the group-level top-line performance. I wrote in the Jan '25 piece that its "Speedcat sneakers" have "become very popular with consumers." This particular collection was well-received in Asia during the last quarter.

Also, it noted at the results briefing that "the Training category remained resilient and delivered healthy growth." The specific financial contribution wasn't quantified by management. But my prior write-up did indicate that the "training apparel category boasted superior margins as compared to the company as a whole."

Anticipating Narrowing Revenue Contraction And Losses In 2026

PMMAF's FY25 turnover decline and operating losses amounted to -8.1% (constant-currency basis) and -€166 million, respectively.

It's guiding for an EBIT of -€0.1 billion (mid-point) and a negative "low-to-mid single digit" sales growth for the new year. I believe this is achievable based on the favorable 4th quarter read-throughs.

The company's earnings call indicated that its "training footwear franchise" experienced "sellouts at the beginning of the year." Customers weren't deterred by this line's industry-leading "price points of around about EUR 250." Management spoke specifically about "HYROX, a key partnership that we have extended last year sees increasing popularity."

In the preceding section, I've already highlighted the "training category's" outperformance in 4Q2025. This segment's future looks bright.

SA News reported in late 2025 that PMMAF will be the "official global apparel and footwear partner for all HYROX events" until the end of the decade. The latter was described as "the world’s fastest-growing fitness sport."

Separately, the enterprise's future profitability should be boosted by sales mix optimization.

As mentioned earlier, PMMAF is reducing its exposure to wholesale, which is lower-margin than "Direct-To-Consumer/DTC."

Its goal is to shift the DTC-to-wholesale contribution ratio from the current 33-67 to 40-60 going forward. I think that will translate into a rising company-wide GPM over time.

New Minority Investor Should Bring Positive Changes

ANPDY has proposed to take "a 29.06% stake in Puma from the French Pinault family's Artémis holding company" according to a late-January news article. This transaction is expected to be concluded before the year ends.

My initiation piece had referred to the acquiror as "the largest domestic sportswear company in China."

I believe PMMAF will benefit from this development.

Puma has the potential to broaden its presence in the Chinese market with ANPDY's support.

The proportion of PMMAF's revenue derived from the PRC is a modest high-single digit percent. ANPDY previously invested in Amer Sports (AS) in '19. AS's China sales contribution subsequently surged from 2010's 8% to 2025's 28%. You can imagine a similar growth path materializing at Puma too.

Also, the company can achieve margin enhancement thanks to a stronger bargaining power post-investment.

As an example, Shenzhou International (SHZHY) is a major apparel supplier for both ANPDY and PMMAF, according to Nikkei Asia. The trailing 12 months revenue for Puma/ANTA combined is $19 billion. It's reasonable for me to assume that they can negotiate for more substantial bulk purchasing discounts with SHZHY.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

PMMAF might execute poorly on the ongoing corporate reorganization program. This can possibly lead to slower growth and wider losses.

The synergies between PMMAF and ANPDY may not be fully realized. There's no guarantee that the management teams of both companies will work well together.

My Final Remarks

I've retained a "Buy" rating for this name.

PMMAF is now trading at a forward Enterprise Value-to-Sales of 0.8 times (source: S&P Capital IQ).

The average consensus next 12 months EV/S for ANPDY, Nike (NKE), and Adidas (ADDYY) is 1.9x.

The stock's revenue-based multiple could re-rate by +50% (1.2/0.8) benchmarked against its own five-year trading mean of 1.2x.

I see catalysts like the attainment of the '26 guide and a value-enhancing partnership with its new shareholder.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Do Investors Keep Buying Century Bonds?
data.description:
By Joakim Book

Investors buying 100-year bonds are making a bold wager on inflation, monetary policy, and financial stability.

Every few years, the century bond returns - not exactly with a bang, but with a new, shiny calculation involved.

As reported by the FT and Bloomberg last month, Google's parent company (GOOG, GOOGL) has been busy tapping the bond market for everything from 3-year to 50-year bonds in several major markets (US, Switzerland, UK). Now it's trying for the ultimate prize: a century bond.

Century bonds are exactly what they sound like: a bond, often issued by a government or a long-lived institution like a university, that runs for a hundred years, often at interest rates somewhat above prevailing market rates or reference rates. For the issuer, they make a ton of sense, generationally and actuarially: They receive funds for investments right now, lock in financing costs for a long time, and face no financing rollover risk.

It's more of a puzzle why buyers show up for these issuances, especially since the market participants have very recent examples of being seriously burned. As a bondholder of extremely long-dated bonds, you're always living in financial terror, waiting for rates to rise and inflict multiplied damage on the market value of your investment. Since bond prices move inversely with interest rates, the effects are more pronounced the further into the future - the longer-dated - the bond is. The loss of market value on a hundred-year bond when interest rates increase is far greater than on a ten- or two-year bond. When it does, this "dangers of duration," as FT journalist Robin Wigglesworth has called it, completely undermines your finances for decades on end.

The last two times century bonds popped out of academic obscurity, they got a well-deserved bad rap. In the 1990s, several large companies tried them - and locked in steep and expensive rates in the five-percent region while interest rates kept falling toward zero for decades. In the late 2010s, with ZIRP dominating the world's financial markets and trillions of mostly government debt traded at negative yield, we started seeing new players dusting off this old idea, since money now was just so cheap to be had: Universities like UPenn, Virginia, Oxford, and Rutgers took in funds for a hundred years in the two to four percent range. Then, the opportunistic governments (including Ireland, Belgium, Mexico, Argentina) also successfully placed century bonds at eye-poppingly low rates. The most extreme participant was Austria, whose perfectly timed century bonds - of 2.1 percent in 2017, and then 0.85 percent and even zero percent right on the cusp of the 'rona inflation - saved its taxpayers a fortune.

Logic alone dictates that if you sell debt due in 2120 for zero percent or 0.85 percent a year, and then CPI (and thus your incomes and tax revenues) rise to upward of 10 percent for a few years, you're doing great. Indeed, bond math logic made that exercise even more painful, with some of these placements trading at cents on the euro a few years after issuance, vaporizing bondholders' money.

Enter Big Tech: The AI Investment Needs Meet Underwater Pension Systems

With Alphabet/Google's placements in November, and now its Swiss franc and sterling placements, it's the first time a big tech company has dared to come back to this perilous market since the 1990s.

What's so odd about this hunger for long-duration is that in the 2010s and during the pre-inflation pandemic years, at least bond investors collectively were starved for yield, ready to do anything to eke out a few extra basis points of return. In 2026, there are still positive yields to be had. The mystery, then, is not why Google issued but why investors rushed in.

The $20-billion USD placement finalized at Treasury yields+95 bps, while the GBP bond of £5.5, about $7.5 billion, placed at 120 basis points above gilts; the £1 billion century bond was ten times oversubscribed, according to Reuters, and carries a coupon of 6.125 percent until 2126. With the fairly recent history of century bond investors burning obscene amounts of money on mistaken duration bets, the question remains: Why were these long-maturity debt placements massively oversubscribed, at rate spreads of only about a hundred basis points above safe assets?

Three candidate explanations.

First, defined-benefit pension funds are extremely hungry for long-dated debt. Reinsurers and life insurance providers, too. They have long-dated liabilities that rise and fall in (net present) value with interest rates; owning equally long-dated assets compensates somewhat for that. "Strong demand from UK pension funds and insurers has made the sterling market a go-to venue for issuers seeking longer-dated funding," reported Tasos Vossos for Bloomberg.

Second, the large embedded rate leverage is kind of capital-efficient. Bond funds don't buy individual issuances in isolation, but compose a portfolio with a combined desired outcome, constantly micromanaging exposure and duration vis-à-vis a benchmark index. Remarked Marcus Ashworth in an opinion piece:

Buyers of these types of security are looking to dynamically balance the risk of an entire portfolio rather than caring about annual coupon weights. Ultra-long debt allows portfolio managers to barbell their duration needs by buying more 10-year liquid debt rather than illiquid 20- to 50-year maturities.

Which is, perversely, why riding that zero-percent Austrian century bond all the way to the basement between 2020 and 2025 could have been beneficial to certain bond portfolios that paired it with other investments. When rates fall, these bonds soar, letting market participants ride the convexity game in a broad macro rate (and now also FX) game. Under functional monetary regimes with price predictability, long-dated debt is both efficient and common - think about the perpetual British Consol, the financial instrument that made the British Empire.

Plus, there's a ton of capital-intensive bond market efficiency involved in going way out on maturity. Explains quant researcher Navnoor Bawa discussing the news on his Substack:

A one percent decline in long-term rates generates approximately 40-50 percent capital appreciation on century bonds versus 15-20 percent on 30-year debt - making it the most capital-efficient duration exposure available for institutions positioning for lower long-term rates.

Third, this time is different and bond investors have inflation fatigue. Many might just believe that what happened during the pandemic was a once-in-a-generation one-off event and that central bankers will do better going forward. If inflation settles back near its more usual two to three percent, or indeed rates fall back toward zero in an easy/easier monetary policy regime, locking in long real duration today at slightly higher rates might look clever rather than reckless.

This might all work out great for the issuers, and bondholders will celebrate their contribution to the AI revolution... for a few years until we have the next bout of runaway inflation prints. Most likely, the investors in these oversubscribed bonds are setting themselves up for financial troubles, by the sudden jolt of interest rates jumping higher or the slow, gradual erosion of fiat purchasing power.

The spread over sovereign credit says something about the balance sheet strength and the optimism surrounding AI-related big tech investments, certainly with behemoths like Google. Funding operations at these rates show either incredible creditworthiness - with Google and the current wave of AI optimism, at least believable - or devastating mispricing.

Investors got the memo about AI capacity building needs, loud and clear. With collective bond market amnesia, that was about the only meaningful bit they took away.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ross Stores: Strong Q4 Results Stretch The Valuation
data.description:
Previous Coverage

I last reviewed Ross Stores (ROST) in August 2025, and at that time the stock was trading for about $150 per share and appeared slightly overvalued. The stock has oscillated quite a bit since then, dipping to a low of $143 in mid-September, and hitting a new 52-week high following their Q4 earnings announced yesterday, March 3rd, 2026. As indicated on the chart below, ROST has vastly outperformed the overall market as measured by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) during that time period as well, returning almost 40%, while (SPY) has gained around 7%.

With Ross Stores closing out their fiscal year recently, I thought now would be a good time review the company again. We'll also estimate the fair price of the stock based on new free cash flow figures.

Q4 Earnings & Future Guidance

As mentioned above, Ross Stores announced their Q4 2026 earnings on Tuesday, March 3rd. Overall, the earnings report was very strong with shares of the company surging in after-hours trading.

The company posted Non-GAAP earnings per share of $2.00, beating analyst expectations by $0.09, and their own guidance by $0.19 at the midpoint. Top-line revenue came in at $6.64B, which surpassed the consensus by $200M, and was an impressive year-over-year increase of 12.4%. Additionally, same store sales rose a strong 9%, well-above the 3% it increased in the prior year quarter.

Operating income for the quarter climbed from $731M to $814M, an increase of more than $80M, or 11.4%, while the operating margin remained basically flat relative to the same quarter a year ago.

The company also returned a hefty chunk of change to shareholders during the quarter, as it repurchased 1.5M shares for around $262M. However, I do find this somewhat concerning, because in my last article I mentioned they spent $262M on share repurchases too (in the quarter I discussed in that coverage), but were able to buyback approximately 500,000 mores shares for the same cost, just something worth keeping an eye on. For the year, more than 7M shares were bought back for north of $1B, coupled with that, the board of directors began a new share repurchase program valued at more than $2.5B and is expected to take place over the next 24 months.

Also announced in the earnings release was a dividend hike of more than 10%, raising its quarterly dividend from $0.405 to $0.445 per share. This gives the stock a forward dividend yield of around 0.85%, its lowest point in more than four years.

Management also provided guidance for the upcoming quarter, with revenue projected to rise 7.5% at the midpoint and come in around $5.35B, which equates to earnings per share in the range of $1.60 to $1.67, a healthy increase of 11.2% at the midpoint.

Overall, it was an incredibly strong earnings call, which can be read/listened to here, with shares spiking more than 6.5% after the market closed.

Risks/Headwinds

Although Ross Stores turned in an excellent quarter, there are challenges they'll have to contend with if they want to keep this momentum going.

Despite tariffs being struck down by the Supreme Court recently, Trump is never one to give up on something, and this was no different, as his administration just took another avenue. Specifically, Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 which was used to impose 10% tariffs (and later raised to 15%) globally. This Act allows for temporary tariffs for up to a maximum of 150 days. To me, this seems like a "band-aid" on the tariff issue for this administration due to its short timeframe, and I'd expect once it expires we'll see another "longer-term" solution attempted.

Recently, Ross Stores has been investing heavily in new distribution centers to support faster growth and greater inventory flexibility. Management specifically mentioned this in their recent earnings call, and noted consistently high DC costs, with expectations it will pressure margins in next quarter. Obviously expansion is typically a good thing, but doing so responsibly and with a high level of execution is important, as this will help keep CapEx costs under control.

The final item here, isn't necessarily a risk or headwind per se, but just something investors should consider when looking at this company. Ross buys its inventory opportunistically, sometimes outside of the season currently happening (such as buying Christmas items in February). This can lead to inconsistencies for when costs become inventory, and when revenue hits the financials, creating a possible lumpiness effect on earnings. Over time this evens out, but when looking at a specific quarter or time period it can lead to swings in earnings per share.

Valuation

The final piece of this puzzle is to determine if ROST is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so we'll examine it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield and specifically how the current yield compares to its historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have typically been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks today relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data, capturing the rolling 5-year average free cash flow yield for the past 7 years. The valuation chart below juxtaposes ROST's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The key assumption behind this valuation model is that investors have paid a fair price for the stock over the past 5 years, on average.

But since every valuation is an estimate, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range.

As previously mentioned, the stock was moderately overvalued in my last write-up, but the recent run-up in share price has affected the valuation significantly. My model indicates a fair value of $161 based on current FCF numbers, while the actual price sits closer to $212, pointing to a premium valuation right now.

The long-term expected rate of return for ROST comes in around 5.5%, with the expected earnings growth rate of 10.03%, being offset by a return to fair value factor of -5.39% return, while the forward dividend yield of 0.85% marginally effects this figure.

Final Thoughts

In summary, Ross Stores has performed quite well since my last coverage. The company posted a strong quarter with double-digit year-over-year revenue growth and superb same-store sales growth, pair that up with another dividend increase and the company looks to be running on all cylinders. There are some catalysts that could impact the company in the future, but management is ordinarily able to overcome any hurdles. The one hesitation right now is the valuation, as the stock appears to again be trading for a rich valuation. Personally, I think if you're looking to initiate a position, a better entry point will present itself down the road, and if you currently own the stock you've got a quality company in your portfolio, but I wouldn't be adding to it anytime soon without a pullback.

data.title: Rocket Companies: A 30% Firesale Is A Buying Opportunity, Low Mortgage Rates
data.description:
Rocket Companies, Inc. (RKT) was a hot trade as mortgage rates fell throughout much of 2025. Shares soared from $10 early last year to nearly $25 this past January. But the cyclical Financials sector stock has fallen hard, now down by about one-third, despite mortgage rates that briefly dipped below 6%.

I had a "Buy" rating on RKT back in August 2022. Shares are up 82% since then, dividends included. Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating, but some technical damage has been done—more on that later. As it stands, the valuation has turned attractive, now under 15x out-year EPS. Put simply, it's too cheap to ignore.

RKT Has Given Back Gains YoY

Back in February, Rocket reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.11 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $0.09, while revenue of $2.44 billion (up a whopping 105% from the same period a year earlier) was a notable $140 million beat. The Detroit-based company generated $41.6 billion in total net rate lock volume, with a total gain on sale margin of 2.82%.

Shares rose for a third consecutive time following an earnings event, up 2.4% in the session that followed the Q4 numbers. The $47 billion market cap mortgage provider now features a high 63% implied volatility on the equity, with the next earnings date not until May. The options market prices in a high 9.2% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the event.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Rocket delivered a strong Q4, with closed loan volumes jumping 46% sequentially and 70% YoY to $47.3 billion… the best since Q1 2022. The management team took time to detail its long-term strategy, outlining a growth path centered on creating a vertically integrated, AI-driven ecosystem to simplify the homeownership journey. Its acquisitions of Redfin and Mr. Cooper should help achieve its objectives.

Q4 Highlights

In terms of actual results, the company notched nearly $50 billion in loan volume with half the headcount used in early 2022. Underwriting operations have seen substantial efficiency gains, too, with document classification now 75% automated.

So, operations are solid, and the balance sheet is healthy—Rocket has $10.1 billion in cash or other short-term reserves. Looking ahead, the company guided Q1 revenue above expectations at $2.6–$2.8 billion but also projected higher operating expenses, so that’s a risk that bears watching.

Big picture, executives reiterated a bullish outlook for 2026, expecting double-digit mortgage market growth and reaffirming market share goals through 2027. On the call, it was confirmed that Rocket’s market share rose to 5.5% in Q4, a jump from 3.8% a year prior, driven by the aforementioned strategic acquisitions.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to rise sharply this year, with still-solid earnings gains in the out-year and through FY 2028. Revenue should rise to $12-$13 billion annually, but much depends on trends in the mortgage market.

The sellside, meanwhile, has turned mixed lately. There have been a pair of upgrades in the past 90 days compared to a trio of downgrades. I’d like to see improved free cash flow, and I think that will happen after the past year’s heavy investments.

Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $1.14 of FY 2027 operating EPS and apply a below-market 18x price-to-earnings ratio, then shares should trade near $20.50.

I assert that a high-teens P/E makes sense, given the high profit growth rate, but the firm is highly cyclical, which may weigh on the equity valuation. But near 14-15x FY 2027 EPS today is quite cheap.

RKT: Very Low PEG Ratio, Cheap on Out-Year EPS

Key risks for Rocket include higher mortgage rates, perhaps driven by heightened Treasury rate volatility and recalcitrant U.S. inflation. We have already seen the yield on the benchmark 30-year mortgage creep up toward 6.15% from 5.99% recently.

What’s more, housing affordability remains a challenge for lower- and middle-income consumers. Elsewhere, rising competition in the mortgage servicing space could crimp margins in the years ahead. Finally, I hinted at the expense side of the ledger earlier, and it should be monitored as 2026 progresses.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows an unconfirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Thursday, May 7, AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares about 20% to the cheap side on valuation, RKT’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock has dropped below its rising long-term 200-day moving average. Moreover, a trendline break is concerning, and it coincides with a very weak RSI momentum oscillator reading. Another negative factor? The high volume by price profile on the left side of the graph—there’s now a high number of shares traded above the current level. That puts the onus on the bulls to carry RKT above notable overhead supply.

What’s good technically? Take a look at a key horizontal support line just above $15.50. That has historically been a pivot spot—above it, a long play can still make sense. $15.50 is also the 50% retracement of the late 2023 to early 2026 rally.

RKT: Below the 200dma, $15.50 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on RKT. The valuation has turned compelling after the 30%-plus pullback. Technicals are mixed, but not as bearish as you might think, given the pace of the recent drawdown.

data.title: GE Vernova: Cooling AI Energy Demand Could Bust This Stock
data.description:
There’s a lot to like with GE Vernova Inc. (GEV), especially amid the energy-chugging Artificial Intelligence boom. President Donald Trump has taken steps to require AI data center companies to pay for their own energy in an effort to reduce strain on the energy grids that communities across the United States depend on. So far, AI data center builders have been moving rather aggressively to set up energy supplies. GE Vernova, which was spun off from parent company General Electric in 2024, is one of the largest and most diversified electricity companies in the world, active with gas turbines, wind, nuclear, and hydro.

GE Vernova is also one of the largest servicing companies in the electric industry, which should pair well with intensifying efforts by technology firms to set up their own power supplies. Google, Microsoft, and other tech companies will struggle not just in setting up power sources, but also in keeping them running. GE is a world leader in gas turbines. While many data center companies are striving for sustainability, using solar and wind power, for example, AI requires high baseload power 24/7. Gas turbines offer one of the more reliable ways to deliver that power. GEV’s backlog for gas turbines and slot reservations leaped from 62 gigawatts (GW) to 83 GW in Q4 2025. The company is targeting 100 GW by the end of 2026.

I believe investors should be careful with banking too much on energy demand. While I expect GE Vernova to continue to enjoy solid demand going forward, I do suspect surging AI energy will taper off as efficiency is emphasized, particularly in inference. While I’m skeptical of an AI bubble, I won’t rule it out either. These concerns, coupled with a soaring stock price and high valuation, result in a hold rating. GE Vernova as a company likely has bright days ahead, but as an investment, the valuation and risks are too high.

AI Energy Consumption Demand Will Cool

First, it’s important to understand that there are two parts to AI: training and inference. With training, many thousands of processors are used to train data via massive data consumption. GPT-4 is believed to have taken about 50 gigawatts to train, which is enough electricity to power all of San Francisco for 3 days. OpenAI has been mum on the electricity needed for GPT-5, but we can expect it to be substantially higher. (As pushback over AI energy use has intensified, companies have become more tight-lipped.

For now, we can expect energy consumption with frontier models to remain extremely high. My impression of the AI industry is that the race is viewed as something close to winner takes all, with Artificial General Intelligence, meaning AI overtaking most people in most areas, viewed as the ultimate prize. There’s also a race to develop Recursive Self-Improvement (RSI), meaning that the AI model itself can contribute greatly to developing the next AI model. This could create a snowballing avalanche of continued self-improvement that might quickly and greatly speed up the race to AGI.

Maybe AGI turns out to be a mirage, but for now, I expect AI companies to continue to spare no expense when it comes to developing frontier models. Brute power will likely be favored over energy efficiency. The energy-hungry GPUs offered by Nvidia will remain favored in training. Google is an exception to this as the company used its own energy-efficient Tensor chips to train the Gemini 3 model.

Inference is a different story, however, and we’re already seeing Nvidia’s dominance in inference slipping. Once an AI model is trained, the energy cost from training it is basically locked into the model itself. When the model is deployed, it still consumes electricity, but the electricity usage per token processing cost is low. Inference can and will consume a lot of electricity, but that is due to many small users making lots of small requests. Ultimately, inference is believed to be responsible for 80 to 90% of a model’s energy consumption over its useful lifetime.

With inference, efficiency suddenly becomes a lot more important for economic feasibility. NVIDIA has been designing its latest generation chips to consume less energy. With AI inference, the Grace Blackwell chips are 25x more energy efficient compared to the Hopper GPU generation. Meanwhile, companies focused specifically on designing inference chips are likewise delivering large efficiency gains. One company, Taalas, is actually etching LLM models directly into hardware, which the company claims can reduce energy consumption by 10X.

With training, I believe energy consumption will remain a secondary consideration, but with inference, reducing energy consumption will be a priority. Thus, I believe the energy bottleneck will ease more quickly than widely expected. This probably won't materialize until 2027 at the earliest, so GE Vernova will likely continue to enjoy solid sales and revenue growth. Still, with so much future potential priced in due to the AI boom, I'd be wary of investing in GEV.

GEV Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

There’s quite a bit to like with GEV’s fundamentals. YOY revenue growth is solid at 8.97%, and I wouldn’t be surprised if we see this accelerate in the near future as energy investments continue. Levered free cash growth is even better at 70.59%. The 12.83% net income margin and 20.08% gross margin are highly attractive as well.

Current liabilities are high at $40.972 billion, mostly in the form of unearned revenues ($25.774 billion) and accounts payable ($7.264 billion). This is pretty high, especially compared to the $8.935 billion the company has in cash and short-term investments. GEV also has $17.380 billion in receivables. Nothing here strikes me with pressing concern, but I worry that the high debt and receivable levels could become a burden if the economy were to contract or an AI bubble pops. As shown below, unearned revenues have been growing rapidly, likely fueled by the AI energy boom as GEV signs deals.

GEV Unearned Revenues

Where GEV really falls short, in my opinion, is with the P/E valuation. At 49.81 GAAP TTM, GEV has a higher P/E than even many of the most profitable tech stocks. The non-GAAP is similar at 52.00, by the way. The Price/Sales ratio too is rather high at 6.30, well-above the sector median of 2.02. While the growth and margins are strong for an industrial company, they don’t approach the tech sector.

Amid the AI boom, it’s easy to see why investors are excited about it, but P/E and P/S ratios this high are simply too much. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools agree, by the way, assigning an F rating for valuation while giving everything else high marks. My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts also rate this stock a hold overall.

Why I Could Be Wrong

As AI becomes more ingrained in our lives, energy demand due to inference loads could continue to increase even if chips become dramatically more efficient. Further, while the AI race currently is primarily between China and the United States, more countries are looking to jump in. If the EU, Japan, or another party starts making similar large-scale investments, demand for GEV’s solutions and services could soar.

Key Takeaway: A Strong Company, But A Valuation Too Rich

This is a stock I wish I had thought about sooner. In hindsight, it was a perfect AI winner. The energy grid bottleneck is well-known and serious. Gas turbines are one of the best solutions for AI data centers. Other products and services offered by GE Vernova should be in high demand as well. It’s no wonder the stock price has soared, and I can see why investors are so interested in the company.

That said, at the current price, the valuation is simply too high in the face of the considerable risks. Efficiency will become a focus in the inference market for not just Nvidia, but competing chip manufacturers. The path to AI profitability will ultimately rely on energy-efficient inference, I believe, and so I expect energy demand to taper off more quickly than many expect.

data.title: WEG: Exceptional Quality But Limited Upside At 30x Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As a value investor myself, I always feel a bit of discomfort when analyzing stocks like WEG S.A. (WEGZY). The company per se is great, no doubts about that, but I’m never comfortable investing in a stock with a valuation that stretched. Anyhow, I feel I have to leave my personal discomfort on the side and give the company its proper credit. Having said that, and going straight to the point, I think WEG is HOLD at the moment, just like it has been over the past years, honestly.

In my view, WEG is not a typical industrial, since it behaves more like a high-quality compounder inside a capital-intensive sector. The main aspects are its high margins (~19.5% EBIT), ROE above 32%, and consistent global execution, which makes the stock different from the other heavy industrial peers. However, in industries like this, where capital intensity usually erodes returns over time, it’s quite hard to maintain a >30% returns on capital for that long, especially while trying to scale the business.

Anyhow, WEG’s growth, in my view, does not come from a temporary commodity spike or short industrial rebound, but from long-duration global trends. To throw some numbers, WEG’s 5-year revenue CAGR is at ~18.5%, its forward growth is still projected near ~15%, and, on top of that, it’s exposed to electrification, automation, and energy efficiency, which I believe are big names that will be present in the future.

On the other hand, I have to bring back the scaling problem to the talk. Keeping this growth level on a R$ 40+ billion revenue base is no easy feat, demanding continuous reinvestment, precision, and discipline. The proof of that is the recent quarterly earnings, which came more “modest”, and also the 170 basis point decline in ROIC year-over-year. These do not invalidate the HOLD thesis, but are definitely attention points to shareholders to follow the stock up close.

As I write this analysis, WEG trades at 29.92x forward earnings and 21.35x forward EV/EBITDA, which clearly reflects the market confidence in the company’s double-digit expansion. I can understand that confidence, but then again, I strongly believe it also limits the upside potential.

All in all, I’m not challenging the business model, as I think WEG is one to keep for life, if you do not mind the big expectations, of course. The thesis here is just to recognize that, at current pricing, it’s a bit hard to expect something higher than simple earnings growth. In short, if I didn’t have the paper, I wouldn’t rush to buy now. And for those who are already shareholders with a buy-and-hold mindset, just keep them.

WEG In A Few Words

WEG S.A. is a Brazilian-based global manufacturer of electric motors, automation systems, power generation equipment, and transmission and distribution solutions. It started as a Brazilian industrial player and over the course of two decades became a diversified global platform, being present in more than 135 countries.

In the past 12 months, WEG delivered net operating revenues of R$ 40.8 billion, and the business today is exposed to long-term projects involving electrification, energy efficiency, renewable integration, and industrial automation.

Operationally, WEG maintains profitability levels that place it among the most efficient companies in the global industrial sector, with EBIT margins at 19.54%, net margin at 15.5%, ROE at 32.52%, and ROIC at 32.5%. To maintain its considerable growth, WEG reinvests aggressively in innovation, more specifically, R$ 2,910.8 million from cash from operations that totaled R$ 6,451.0 million in 2025.

Financials: Growth, Margins, And Capital Intensity

Growth With Structural Drivers

As mentioned previously, in the past 12 months, WEG reached a revenue of R$ 40.8 billion along with a CAGR of 18.49%. Even though the growth expectations are a bit lower at 15.34%, it’s still within the double digits, which is kind of expected from a premium company like WEG.

However, what stands out to me is that this growth comes from drivers that are inherently aimed at the long run, especially the electrification and energy efficiency verticals, which means that the company is well set for the future. So, regarding goals and plans, there’s not much to discuss; the main concern is whether WEG will keep up the current pace in this course.

At the same time, growth at this size becomes incrementally harder to sustain. Just to put in perspective, if we add the expected growth of 15% to the current revenue base, we would have an addition of something like R$ 6 billion in the course of only one year, and a total revenue of R$ 62 billion by the third year. That’s not impossible and I don’t doubt WEG’s capacity, but when we write the numbers down, it gets clearer that the operation starts to demand top-notch perfection.

Margin Discipline And Capital Allocation

No one can argue that WEG’s profitability is one of the company’s defining characteristics and what sets it apart within the sector. EBIT margin currently stands at 19.54% and net margin in the most recent quarter was 15.5%, turning WEG one of the most efficient industrial players.

However, the latest quarter also showed that the net income declined 6.3%YoY, and ROIC decreased 170 basis points compared to the prior year, standing at 32.5%. While returns are still exceptionally high, shareholders should keep an eye out for any more slowdowns in the near term, which could end up affecting WEG’s impeccable march until now.

Even though WEG is a master at cash reinvesting (roughly 45% of operating cash flow), there comes a point where math hits hard and it’s essentially more demanding to have the same level of ROIC. Some skeptics might even say that WEG already hit a peak and won’t bring any more surprises from now on, which would make it hard for further multiple expansions.

Still, to justify the premium the market is paying right now for the stock, it’s almost mandatory for the company to maintain this level of expenditure. For instance, R&D and innovation expenses reached R$ 1,405.1 million in 2025, equivalent to 3.4% of net revenue, which, in my opinion, is the bare minimum for the company to keep its long term competitive positioning at a global scale.

All in all, WEG is not extracting value from a mature asset base but putting back capital into growth to generate its 32% ROE.

What's Priced In The Stock

All right, now I’m going to throw more numbers so we can forecast some scenarios to try to understand what the market is putting into the stock price.

Right now, WEG trades at 29.92x forward earnings and 21.35x forward EV/EBITDA, with a Price/Sales ratio of 5.46x and Price/Book of 12.79x. Needless to say, those are premium multiples by any standard in the industrial sector, so the market is still clearly expecting sustained growth and capital efficiency.

To frame it in a more tangible way, let’s calculate the implied PEG ratio using the projected growth of 15.34% as a rough estimate for the increase in earnings. Let’s remember that the five-year historical revenue CAGR stands at 18.49%, so, although 15.34% a bit high for normal standards, it’s not really that high when compared to the historical average.

Implied PEG = P/E ÷ Growth

= 29.92 ÷ 15.34

≈ 1.95

This means that, for a capital-intensive industrial company, a PEG around 2.0 shows that investors are paying almost twice the expected growth rate in earnings. Even though this is understandable for a top-quality company such as WEG, this also doesn’t leave much room for setbacks or unplanned events.

However, if we assume a slightly less favorable scenario, we start to see a bit of the “little room for disappointments” case. For instance, if growth slowed down an inch to 13% or if the multiple compressed from 29.92x to 22x, this contraction would represent:

(22 ÷ 29.92 − 1) ≈ −26%

Now, if we take that and spread over three years, we get an annualized valuation setback of ~9%, which, even with a 13% earnings growth, would bring total returns to the mid-single-digit range.

We can also apply this same logic to EV/EBITDA. At 21.35x forward EBITDA, a compression towards 17x would represent a contraction of approximately 20%:

(17 ÷ 21.35 − 1) ≈ −20%

And if EBITDA were to deliver 14% annually but the multiple compresses by 20% over the course of three years, the drop in valuation would offset a meaningful portion of the operating growth.

Wrapping Up

At the end of the day, this all means that WEG has to keep growing at double digits; it has no other choice, because there’s no margin of safety anymore. It must deliver stable margins near 20% and sustained ROIC above 30%, which, then again, are not impossible for WEG, but are also not that easy business-wise.

We all know WEG is a high-quality business that's made for the future, but in my view, with a 30x forward earnings and a 21.35x forward EV/EBITDA, it’s hard to grasp where the risk-return ratio is tilting to, hence my “neutral” stance of HOLD for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Credo: I Can't Believe It Has Now Become A Relative Bargain
data.description:
Credo: Market Still Wants More After Such A Massive Quarter?

These are baffling moments for semiconductor investors. The recent earnings for Nvidia (NVDA) demonstrated to us that, even as the company posted an incredible beat and raise last week, it still wasn’t enough to placate the market, as sentiments for semiconductor stocks have turned decidedly back to the downside.

The bifurcated market in the technology sector has now come for the erstwhile semiconductor winners, after devouring the software companies, as SaaS stocks suffered some of the worst hammering in recent years. Yet, as software mounted a potential bottom last week, the selling pressure percolating across the semiconductor companies implies that the market is ready to take profit, particularly on stocks like Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (CRDO) that is still up more than 90% over the past one year, despite having already declined almost 60% from its 2025 peak.

So before we freak out and think that this semiconductor pullback is going to result in something more structurally bearish, I think we need to be a lot more discerning. And for Credo, come on, this company just posted earnings for its fiscal third quarter with revenue growing at 52% quarter on quarter, while surpassing 200% over the past year. Therefore, I believe some of you could have been dumbfounded about why the market has decided to hit CRDO so hard, despite posting such remarkable results. Especially since there are no signs suggesting that the hyperscaler capital expenditure outlays are tapering anytime soon. Think about it, almost $700 billion of CapEx is expected to be incurred in 2026.

I decided to upgrade CRDO in my previous update, but it seems like the market still needs more convincing. In the current context where there is so much uncertainty going on, it could be befuddling for us to nail down an exact reason why Credo's results haven't yet lifted it from the gloom.

Hyperscaler CapEx Continues To Expand, But Momentum Should Slow

And, in case you thought this was it for AI CapEx, let this chart above sink in, as it should inform you that we have several more years of aggressive spending growth to go before 2030 arrives. So the pertinent question, I believe, isn’t so much about whether the benefits are accruing to Credo. 2026 isn’t even expected to be the peak of capital expenditure spending, but if you look at the above momentum, it does suggest that unless we have even bigger growth rates in 2027, the chances of the gargantuan year-over-year growth that we saw for CRDO in the fiscal third quarter could really have peaked out.

Of course, that’s my conjecture, but the law of large numbers suggests that unless the company seeks out new growth opportunities beyond the AEC levers, it is reasonable to assess that we are not likely to see triple-digit growth anymore from fiscal 2027. And if you look at the guidance that management put out, that is indeed that possible reality the investors must look hard into their eyes and contemplate carefully:

Now turning to our guidance. We currently expect revenue in Q4 of fiscal '26 to be between $425 million and $435 million. We expect Q4 non-GAAP gross margin to be within a range of 64% to 66%. We expect Q4 non-GAAP operating expenses to be between $76 million and $80 million, and we expect Q4 diluted weighted average share count to be approximately 197 million shares. These expectations are based on the current tariff regime, which remains fluid. As we look ahead to fiscal '27, we expect sequential revenue growth in the mid-single digits, leading to more than 50% year-over-year growth. - Credo Q3 FY2026 earnings call.

On the guidance for fiscal Q4, Credo still expects to deliver more than 150% increase in revenue. But as I mentioned earlier, this momentum isn’t expected to carry on, which is why there are concerns about whether the AEC market could be facing upside constraints that may not have been fully contemplated when the stock was trading at a significant premium over its semiconductor peers back in late 2025.

Credo: Needs To Prove That Massive Growth Can Sustain

So let me show you some insights from the wisdom of the market. Quite clearly, we could see that there’s a bearish reversal that stemmed further upside on CRDO’s against the semiconductor peers (SMH) back in December. That was really aligned with the worries during those times when co-packaged optics percolated into the market narrative, as investors now need to assess whether this could be another potentially significant, and monumental technological transition that could leave CRDO vulnerable to losing market share.

While management has emerged confidently in early 2026, releasing its preliminary results to help shield the stock from further downside, it doesn’t seem to have done enough to assure investors that 2027 could be a tougher year. However, worries about an imminent disruption suggest that the market could be pricing in too much downside, as the reliability, TCOs, and adaptability for scale-out networking don't seem quite ready for prime time.

And we are talking about hyperscalers after all, who are really the most important customers right now for Credo, as they focus most of their targeting on scale-out networks. But could there be a case that can be made for CPOs after all?

Possible, but potentially benefiting scale-up more from 2027, certainly not now. And for scale-out, the technical challenges are not expected to be resolved in the near term, which also aligns with what management has mentioned about, when it comes to customers demanding reliability as one of the key factors, indicating that they will use copper as long as possible.

So these are very much lined up. So based on the upside potential for 2026, and as for fiscal 2027, I believe the possibility for further upgrades is still likely, given the intense competition right now between the hyperscalers and big technology companies, that will behoove them to avoid making unforced errors, like adopting a technology that may not yet be ready for rapid scaling, something that management is reminding us why these guys will want to stick with copper for as long as possible:

Our AEC product line once again delivered strong growth, driven by existing customers and new wins, including our fifth hyperscaler. Demand is accelerating across both hyperscalers and emerging Neocloud providers. We continue to believe the industry is early in its AEC adoption. As AI clusters scale, reliability and power efficiency have become the primary design constraints. AECs are now the de facto standard for intra-rack and rack-to-rack connectivity up to 7 meters, increasingly displacing laser-based optical modules. - Credo Q3 FY2026 earnings call.

These high level assurances should have placated the market, right? What else is the market looking for, as the stock fell more than 14% yesterday (March 3)? Worried about the lack of customer diversification? I mean, after all, almost 90% of its revenue base emanated from just three customers. So you can understand why if there was going to be a potential slowdown from fiscal 2027, which we saw in the guidance, then perhaps the growth multiples reflect such a caution.

Although it’s generally true that the company has built a pretty remarkable platform that transcends beyond just cables, the fact that the growth levers have been based on the opportunity set driven by its AEC, then I think some caution is necessary, although it appears to me that the market has taken it way too far than what is required. Moreover, I think management has given us some useful insights to chew on, as the company prognosticates that we could see zero-flap optics gaining a firmer foothold from fiscal 2027, providing a much-needed ballast onto the AEC growth pillar. So I do see that there’s a diversification in growth levers that the company could still continue to pull and execute.

And profitable execution has been very much the hallmark for Credo in the past two fiscal years, even though I think the market is now getting a little bit angsty about whether similar momentum can be expected from the subsequent fiscal years. As we can see from the revised estimates, it does suggest that growth rates could fall further behind its previous gangbusters metrics, even as it grows into its nascent opportunities with the hyperscalers.

But with free cash flow margins expected to stay close to the 40% mark through the 2028 fiscal year, I don’t expect any decline to be structurally bearish. Therefore, when the valuations come to a more sustainable level that is broadly in line with the semiconductor average, I think that’s where we could see buyers returning with more conviction than before.

Buy Or Sell CRDO Stock?

And I think I could say that’s precisely what I am observing right now. CRDO’s valuation multiple has dropped to less than 22x, coming in below the semiconductor median of >26x. So we have the first criterion, which is the market has really battered this stock into submission.

Next, when we take a look at the price action, we are now testing another critically important moving average, the 20 month MA, which is the red line in my chart. In past instances where the market tape coincided with that level of support, buyers have consistently come back to help the stock bottom out, which you can see in green from the above.

I haven’t seen anything yet suggesting a bullish reversal is now decisive, but we are still early, and since this is a monthly chart, more cautious investors could allow more time for the price action to play out over the rest of March before deciding further.

However, I’m confident that the company has executed incredibly well, and CPO is also not expected to feature heavily in the hyperscaler buildout for the current and subsequent fiscal year. What’s also critical is that Wall Street has not gone crazily FOMO based on fiscal 2027 estimates, as the model is a slower growth cadence.

So it looks like the market is simply pricing in these ambiguities as they play out, but allowing their emotion to take the hammering way too far than what it really deserves.

I see a glorious opportunity for semiconductor investors who are keen on parlaying the recent weakness into a high quality play, and before the rest of the market realizes it.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Nvidia Q4 Earnings Call: What It Means For SMH
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) delivered another record quarter driven by AI data center demand and guided higher. The results reinforce continued strength across the semiconductor and AI ecosystem.

What Did Nvidia Report?

Nvidia reported record Q4 revenue of approximately $68.1 billion and earnings per share of about $1.62, both ahead of expectations, driven primarily by continued strength in its data center segment. Data center revenue remained the dominant contributor as hyperscalers and enterprises accelerated AI infrastructure deployment. Management emphasized ongoing AI compute demand and the ramp of next-generation platforms as key focal points during the call.

Why NVDA Earnings Call Matters for the Market

  • Signals sustained AI infrastructure spending across the semiconductor ecosystem.
  • Highlights continued pricing power and scale benefits in advanced compute.
  • Provides insight into enterprise and hyperscaler AI capex trends.

Nvidia’s results serve as a read-through on broader semiconductor demand, particularly in high-performance computing, advanced packaging, memory, and AI networking.

What This Means for SMH

These results reinforce trends relevant through the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX), particularly exposure to:

  • Leading-edge chip designers powering AI training and inference.
  • Foundries and manufacturing capacity supporting advanced nodes.
  • Memory and high-bandwidth memory providers critical for AI workloads.
  • AI infrastructure suppliers benefiting from continued hyperscale investment.

Nvidia’s earnings underscore the central role of semiconductors in enabling AI model development and deployment. As AI workloads scale, demand extends beyond a single company to the broader chip ecosystem represented across SMH and SMHX.

Nvidia’s Outlook for the Next Quarter

Nvidia guided revenue to approximately $76–79.5 billion for the upcoming quarter, above consensus expectations.

Risks Opportunities
Concentration among large hyperscale customers Continued AI training and inference expansion
Export restrictions and geopolitical pressures Enterprise AI adoption beyond hyperscalers
Cyclicality in semiconductor capital spending Next-generation platform ramps driving incremental demand

Who Should Be Paying Attention

Investors focused on AI infrastructure trends or monitoring semiconductor demand cycles may find these earnings particularly relevant.

Important Disclosures

Holdings will vary for the SMH ETF and its corresponding Index. For a complete list of holdings in the ETF, please click here.

Holdings will vary for the SMHX ETF and its corresponding Index. For a complete list of holdings in the ETF, please click here.

data.title: Ampco-Pittsburgh: Record Orders, Defense Pull, And Margin Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Ampco-Pittsburgh Corporation (AP) makes metal components such as tubes, pipes, coils, and cast-iron products. The market has been bullish on the ticker after management made some bold decisions to exit unprofitable business lines, including a cast-roll facility in the UK, where legacy contracts weighed down on financials, leaving no way out other than putting the subsidiary into administration. This will create short-term pressure on earnings as AP writes down the assets tied to the discontinued operations. The market welcomed the news, given the profitability benefits.

Adding to the momentum is management's optimism on the back of a surge in orders in Q3'25. Last month, AP confirmed the continuation of this momentum, with the Air and Liquid segment reporting record orders in January. Management cited the US military sector as a key driver. This exposure to the US Defense sector could offer a tailwind as geopolitical tensions add urgency to the Pentagon's projects, including missile and ship building, where the need for AP's products is critical.

Revenue Momentum

In Q3'25, AP's revenue stood at $108 million, up $11.8 million or 12% YoY. This increase was driven by a 39% surge in Centrifugal Pump sales, adding $3.6 million to Q3'25 revenue. Centrifugal pumps are key components in centrifugal motors used to pump water, often found on large ships.

The US president signed an executive order to enhance the productivity of the US ship-building capabilities, particularly for defense applications. Washington's renewed commitment to shipbuilding creates a positive catalyst for AP sales.

Sales ($ 000s) Q3'25 Q3'24 $ Change % Change
Forged and cast mill rolls $ 66,455 $ 63,833 $ 2,622 4%
FEP $ 5,012 $ 3,370 $ 1,642 49%
Air handling systems $ 11,169 $ 8,853 $ 2,316 26%
Heat exchange coils $ 12,483 $ 10,824 $ 1,659 15%
Centrifugal pumps $ 12,890 $ 9,286 $ 3,604 39%
Total $ 108,009 $ 96,166 $ 11,843 12%

The company also cited the pharmaceutical sector as a revenue tailwind in recent quarters. Air handling systems were the second-highest growth driver, adding $2.3 million to the quarter's sales. The president has been pushing for more local drug production.

Profitability - Progress Noticed by Market

In the nine months ended September 2025, AP reported net losses attributed to common shareholders of -$8.3 million, down from a -$2.7 million loss. However, a significant portion of these losses is attributed to one-time items. In the depreciation & amortization line, there are expenses tied to early retirement of certain assets, particularly those tied to discontinued operations. There are other expenses tied to severance and restructuring expenses.

The adjusted Net Income reconciliation table below adjusts for these non-cash, non-recurring items, in addition to stock-based compensation (SBC).

Millions $ 9M September 2025
GAAP Net Income Attributed to Shareholders ($8.4)
Add: Exit Charges + Employee Retention (p.31) $9.1
SBC $0.972
Non-GAAP Adjusted Net Income Attributed to Shareholders $1.65

In Q4'24, AP adjusted down the estimate for its asbestos litigation liability, recording the benefit in the income statement. Since this is a non-recurring item that doesn't reflect the underlying performance, we exclude this benefit in the non-GAAP net income calculation. The asbestos benefit stood at ~$4 million (p.19), recorded in Q4'24. Deducting this figure from the quarter's GAAP net income of $3.1 million implies an adjusted net loss of $0.9 million.

On a TTM basis, adjusted net loss stood at -$227,000. That -$0.01 per share TTM adjusted EPS, based on a share count of 20,326,389 shares.

Outlook

Investors should expect GAAP net losses to deteriorate significantly in Q4'25 after the UK is put into administration, an event that will trigger a one-time, non-cash expense of $43 million to $45 million in the quarter. For a small-cap company, these are very steep GAAP losses.

However, looking beyond these non-cash items, we should expect improved profitability in Q4 and FY'26. Management said that the UK exit is expected to increase EBITDA by $7 to $8 million annually ($7.5 million at midpoint). Assuming a pro-rated profitability tailwind, we should expect an EBITDA increase of $1.6 million in Q4'25 (since the UK exit was completed in mid-October).

As a small company, AP's earnings could be volatile. I'm seeing large contracts on the back of procurement shifts, particularly from the defense industry, swinging earnings of smaller companies with exposure to the industry.

Assuming a continued momentum through Q4'25, with adjusted EBITDA growth rate sustained at 15% sequentially, and adding the $1.6 million tailwind from the UK de-consolidation, Q4'25 adjusted EBITDA forecast stands at roughly $12.2 million.

The reconciliation with adjusted net income is as follows:

Million $ Q4'25
Adjusted EBITDA 12.2
Deduct: Interest Expense $3
Depreciation & Amortization $4.7
SBC 0.3
Adjusted net income before tax $4.2
Effective Tax Rate 10%
Adjusted net income $3.8

The table above assumes no change in the company's debt structure in Q4'25 and no change in interest expenses. The adjusted Depreciation & Amortization of Q3'25 is used as an estimate for Q4'25. A $0.3 million SBC assumption is consistent with historical averages. Finally, a 10% is a working figure that captures state and international taxes, weighed against operating loss carry forwards. Adding the 3.8 million Q4'25 forecast to the 9M adjusted net income estimate of ~$1.7 million adds up to a FY'25 adjusted net income estimate of $5.5 million, or a full-year EPS of $0.26 based on 20,326,389 shares outstanding.

To simplify calculations, I use Q4'25 adjusted net income as a conservative run rate for FY'26. This estimate is conservative because it doesn't incorporate further growth in sales or margins. Based on a $15.2 million annualized net income run rate, FY'26 EPS would stand at $0.74/share, translating to a forward 2026 P/E ratio of a mere $11.6x.

The asbestos litigation liability reserves muddy the valuation picture. AP estimates roughly $186 million in payments related to alleged past asbestos violations. The actual outlays could be higher or lower. Still, using management's estimates as a working figure, AP's forward 2025 EV/EBITDA stands at 8.64x below the material's segment's median. The EV/EBITDA ratio is based on the $12.2 million adjusted EBITDA forecast for Q4'25 above, the actual EBITDA figures for 9M'25 reported last November, and an EV estimate of 330 million (including the asbestos liabilities).

Final Thoughts and Risks

AP made a tough decision to put its unprofitable UK subsidiary into administration. Investors should expect steep GAAP losses when the company reports Q4'25 results. However, this is offset by a better cost structure. I expect AP to report GAAP profits in 2026. Looking at valuation, the company seems undervalued.

The main downside risk is the lumpiness of demand and orders. AP benefited from what seems like a renewed market activity on the back of the current administration's manufacturing localization push. With such political exposure, exacerbated by exposure to the defense sector, this bullish hypothesis does lose some of its luster. Defense demand is subject to budget constraints and changes, and revenue tailwinds might not be as sustainable as implied in this piece.
